text: Today I am going to show the robotic assisted pelvic lymphadenectomy. And in addition to the wide balance and focusing you do, you know because we have a three dimensional image here, it's a binocular vision, we need to use this device to do three dimensional image.

The surgeon uses a V-care uterine manipulator instead of the Karl Stoll retrain manipulator, avoiding insertion into the endometrial canal due to malignancy concerns. Robotic instruments with endo-risk technology are visible, including needle drivers and Maryland dissectors, capable of 360-degree rotation. The robotic-assisted ports and trocars are shown, measuring eight millimeters in diameter.

The surgeon prepares the patient for robotic surgery by positioning the robot close to the patient. The camera port is placed after marking the target organ, specifically the uppermost part of the fundus of the uterus. The port is positioned at least two palm breadths or eighteen centimeters above the target line due to the length of the robotic instruments. The surgeon uses the HACCP technique with a special trocar featuring a balloon catheter.

The surgeon inserts the trocar and inflates the bulb to secure it in place, ensuring it remains stable. The port placements are adjusted to maintain a distance of at least one palm breadth to prevent the robotic arms from collapsing, positioned approximately fifteen centimeters from the camera port and about one centimeter below its level.

The surgeon places a supraumbilical camera port for robotic surgery, ensuring it is positioned fifteen centimeters laterally to form a quadrangle known as the 'no port zone.' The camera features two telescopes for binocular vision. The surgeon infiltrates the area with zallocane and bupecane before inserting two robotic ports labeled R-I and R-II, using an ATMM port.

The surgeon places two robotic ports, R-one on the right side and R-two on the left side, on the abdominal wall. The robotic instruments pivot around a small black thick band, ensuring precise movement. The camera port is docked first, secured by a magnetic strip that produces a sound upon engagement, followed by docking of all instruments. The robot controls both the instruments and the trocar, preventing slippage.

The robotic system is docked, with the patient cart securely positioned. The surgeon uses hand sensors to control the robotic arms, which include a camera clutch, monopolar cautery, and bipolar cautery. The instruments are arranged around the surgical field, providing a clear view as the uterus is lifted, showcasing the surgical site in a two-dimensional view on the screen.
